PMC2911553.md:Warfarin, a coumarin derivative, exerts its anticoagulant effect by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), hence preventing vitamin K 2,3- epoxide reducing to vitamin K hydroquinone which is essential for the activation of vitamin K-dependent clotting factors [FII (or (pro)thrombin), FVII, FIX, and FX]. It is the most commonly used oral anticoagulant for patients with mechanical heart valve replacement (MHVR) to prevent thromboembolism. However, due to the narrow therapeutic index and the wide inter-individual variation, it usually takes a long time to reach the target international normalized ratio (INR). To minimize the adverse events and to shorten the time to reach the target INR, algorithms including patients' clinical characteristics and genetic polymorphism information have been explored.
PMC3125052.md:The use of oral anticoagulants has become one of the most common therapies to treat patients suffering from thromboembolic disorders. Warfarin/Acitrom and related coumarins are widely used in thromboembolic prophylaxis. The subtherapeutic and supratherapeutic use of these drugs is associated with an increased risk of clot formation and bleeding, respectively. This is because of the narrow therapeutic index of these anticoagulants. There is much concern to ensure improvement in the safety of oral anticoagulant therapy, and many efforts have been made for this purpose. However, still, the risks for clotting and bleeding subsequent to oral anticoagulant therapy remain significantly high.[[1](#CIT1),[2](#CIT2)] Warfarin and Acitrom are two widespread oral anticoagulant drugs that are used to maintain the International Normalized Ratio (INR) within the therapeutic range. However, the dose requirements of these anticoagulants vary between individuals as well as within an individual, depending on the dietary intake or as a result of variations in pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics, compliance, etc.[[3](#CIT3)] In recent years, common genetic variations in vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKOR) have been discovered to significantly influence the oral anticoagulant maintenance dose requirements. Acitrom (or Warfarin) inhibits vitamin K epoxide reductase,[[4](#CIT4)] specifically the VKORC1 subunit,[[5](#CIT5),[6](#CIT6)] thereby diminishing the available vitamin K and vitamin K hydroquinone in the tissues. Interindividual variations in the VKORC1-1639 G>A gene polymorphism has been linked to differences in the dose requirements for oral anticoagulants, A recent study compared the weight-normalized oral anticoagulant dose requirements for Chinese and Malays with that for Indians,[[7](#CIT7)] and reported the oral anticoagulant dose requirement in Indians to be higher than that in the Chinese. In this study, most Indians belonged to South India. The Indian population comprises multiple ethnic groups, and there is a need to obtain data from other regions. Therefore, we carried out a pilot study in the North Indian population to find out the relationship between the VKORC1-1639 G>A genotypes and maintenance doses of oral anticoagulants for therapeutically stable INR values in patients taking Acitrom after valve replacement surgery.
PMC3175513.md:Oral anticoagulants are extensively used in clinical practice to prevent and treat thromboembolic disorders [[1](#b1)]. The coumarins are the most used class of oral anticoagulants, to which warfarin belongs. The effect of this drug is influenced by several factors. Its pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics vary with bodyweight, diet, gender, drugs and genetic factors [[2](#b2)]. The dose to achieve the target INR ranges from an average of 4 mg to 80 mg weekly [[3](#b3)].
PMC3175513.md:Single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in the cytochrome P450 complex (*CYP2C9*) and vitamin K epoxide reductase (*VKORC1*) genes affect the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of coumarins, respectively, and are strongly associated with warfarin dose. CYP2C9*2 (rs1799853) in exon 3 (430C>T, Arg144Cys) and CYP2C9*3 (rs1057910) in exon 7 (1075A>C, Iso359Leu) are associated with change in dose, since carriers of these alleles require a lower dose of warfarin [[4](#b4)–[6](#b6)]. The −1639G>A (rs9923231 – at promoter) and 1173C>T (rs9934438 – at intron 1) polymorphisms in the *VKORC1* gene are associated with a reduction of approximately 30% in warfarin dose [[7](#b7)]. In contrast, 3730G>A (rs7294 – 3'UTR) polymorphism, in the same gene, has been associated with an increased dose of the anticoagulant [[7](#b7), [8](#b8)]. Polymorphism 1347C>T (rs2108622) in *CYP4F2*[[9](#b9), [10](#b10)] and 494C>T (rs5896) in *F2*[[11](#b11), [12](#b12)] have also been associated with warfarin dose. Several studies proposed different pharmacogenetic algorithms [[13](#b13)–[19](#b19)] to determine the individual dose for each patient, but until now, most of them explained up to a maximum of 60% of warfarin dose variation.
PMC3384479.md:# Vitamin K antagonists in children with heart disease: height and VKORC1 genotype are the main determinants of the warfarin dose requirement
PMC3384479.md:Managing vitamin K antagonist (VKA) therapy is challenging in children because of a narrow therapeutic range and wide inter- and intra-individual variability in dose response. Only a few small studies have investigated the effect of nongenetic and genetic factors on the dose response to VKAs in children. In a cohort study including 118 children (median age 9 years; range, 3 months-18 years) mostly with cardiac disease, we evaluated by multivariate analysis the relative contribution of nongenetic factors and VKORC1/CYP2C9/CYP4F2 genotypes on warfarin (n = 83) or fluindione (n = 35) maintenance dose and the influence of these factors on the time spent within/above/below the range. The results showed that height, target international normalized ratio and VKORC1 and CYP2C9 genotypes were the main determinants of warfarin dose requirement, accounting for 48.1%, 4.4%, 18.2%, and 2.0% of variability, respectively, and explaining 69.7% of the variability. Our model predicted the warfarin dose within 7 mg/wk in 86.7% of patients. None of the covariates was associated with the time spent above or below the international normalized ratio range. Whether this model predicts accurately the effective maintenance dose is currently being investigated.
PMC3384479.md:In pediatric patients, vitamin K antagonists (VKAs) are mainly used to prevent thromboembolism after cardiac valve replacement, total cavopulmonary connection, dilated cardiomyopathy, coronary aneurysms after Kawasaki disease, or, less frequently, extra-cardiac diseases.^1–3^ VKA therapy is challenging in children, because VKAs have a narrow therapeutic range and considerable inter- and intra-individual dose-response variability.^2^ This variability is partly explained by age and other demographic, clinical, and environmental factors such as comedications. In the last decade, an increasing number of genetic variations affecting VKA pharmacodynamics and/or pharmacokinetics were found to have a major impact on the VKA dose in adults.^4–15^ These genetic variations are found in single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) in *VKORC1*, *CYP2C9*, and *CYP4F2*.^4–15^ VKAs exert their anticoagulant effect by inhibiting the enzyme vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1), thereby preventing vitamin K recycling and vitamin K–dependent carboxylation of the coagulation factors II, VII, IX, and X.^16^ SNPs located in the VKORC1 gene (*VKORC1*) have the largest effect on the response to VKAs. More specifically, patients carrying the −1639 G > A SNP (rs9923231) located in the functional promoter of *VKORC1* require substantially lower doses than do wild-type patients, and a gene-dose effect has been reported for this genetic variant.^5,17^ The pharmacokinetics of warfarin and other coumarin derivatives depend mainly on the activity of cytochrome P450 2C9 (CYP2C9), a microsomal hepatic enzyme responsible for oxidation of these drugs to inactive metabolites. The effect of CYP2C9 on non-coumarin VKAs such as fluindione is unclear.^18^ Two common SNPs in the *CYP2C9* gene, *CYP2C9***2* (rs1799853) and *CYP2C9***3* (rs1057910), are associated with decreased CYP2C9 catalytic activity compared with wild-type *CYP2C9***1*, and therefore, patients carrying at least one variant allele require lower doses of coumarin derivatives than do wild-type patients.^19^ More recently, an SNP (rs2108622) in the *CYP4F2* gene encoding cytochrome 4F2 involved in vitamin K metabolism was shown to be associated with higher warfarin dose requirements.^10,12,20^ Overall, genetic factors accounted for 30%-40% of the dose variability in white adults.^6,7,9,10,12,13,21–23^
PMC3384479.md:Herein we report the results of a cohort study of 118 children (age 3 months to 18 years) who were followed in pediatric cardiology departments while receiving long-term VKA treatment. Our primary objective was to determine the relative contributions of nongenetic and genetic factors (*VKORC1*, *CYP2C9*, and *CYP4F2*) on warfarin or fluindione dose requirements in this cohort. Our secondary objective was to evaluate the potential influence of these factors on the time spent within, above, and below the international normalized ratio (INR) range.
PMC3384479.md:Between September 2009 and December 2010, we enrolled 120 unrelated patients. Two patients receiving acenocoumarol were not analyzed. The study population comprised 55 girls and 63 boys including more than 90% white and the median age was 9.0 years (range, 3 months-18 years). Of the 118 patients, 83 received warfarin and 35 received fluindione. [Table 1](#T1) displays the mean VKA dose by age group and VKA type. In the 83 patients on warfarin (median age, 9.0 years), the mean weekly maintenance dose was 23.2 ± 15.0 mg (range, 3.5-84 mg), which corresponded to 0.93 ± 0.55 mg/kg (range, 0.19-3.47 mg/kg). In the 35 patients on fluindione (median age, 11.0 years), the mean weekly maintenance dose was 123.1 ± 52.2 mg (range, 45-250 mg), corresponding to 4.07 ± 2.68 mg/kg (range, 1.15-12.19 mg/kg). When the VKA dose was expressed relative to body weight (mg/kg), we found that very young patients, especially those younger than 3 years, required higher weekly doses than older patients ([Table 1](#T1)). Two patients were younger than 1 year of age (12 and 15 weeks).
PMC3384479.md:| Fluindione dose (n = 35) | n = 1 | n = 2 | n = 12 | n = 12 | n = 8 |
PMC3384479.md:The main characteristics of the 118 patients receiving warfarin or fluindione are shown in [Table 2](#T2). Overall, 38 patients had total cavopulmonary connection with a target INR of 2.2. Forty-nine patients had a target INR of 2.5; of these, 15 had aortic valve replacement, 16 had dilated cardiomyopathy, 6 had coronary aneurysms after Kawasaki disease, and 12 had extracardiac diseases (2 strokes with cyanotic congenital heart disease, 2 pulmonary arterial hypertension, 4 cardiac arrhythmias, 1 lupus, 1 antiphospholipid syndrome, 1 thrombosis with drepanocytosis, and 1 thrombosis with histiocytosis); 31 patients had mitral valve replacement with a target INR of 3.3.
PMC3384479.md:|   | Warfarin (n = 83) |  | Fluindione (n = 35) |  |
PMC3384479.md:The distribution of the *VKORC1*, *CYP2C9*, and *CYP4F2* genotypes is shown on [Table 2](#T2). The *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* allele frequencies did not differ from those found in the general white population ([Table 2](#T2)).^9,10^ No significant deviation from the Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium was observed for any of the SNPs (*P* > .05; [Table 2](#T2)). The allele frequency of the *CYP4F2* gene was as expected in the warfarin-treated group, with no significant deviation from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium. In the fluindione-treated group, allele frequency differed from that in the general population and there was a significant deviation from Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium, probably because of the small sample size (n = 35).
PMC3384479.md:### Influence of genetic and nongenetic variables on maintenance dose in fluindione-treated patients
PMC3384479.md:The *VKORC1* genotype was the only variable significantly associated with the weekly fluindione maintenance dose (*P* = .0363; [Table 2](#T2)). Because the *CYP4F2* allele frequency differed from that in the general population, this variable was not entered into the statistical analysis. None of the nongenetic factors was significantly associated with the weekly fluindione maintenance dose ([Table 2](#T2)).
PMC3384479.md:The fluindione-treated patients (n = 31) spent 81.4% ± 15.7% (range, 36.5%-100%) of the time within the INR range, 11.4% ± 11.6% (range, 0%-36.7%) above the INR range, and 7.7% ± 11.6% (range, 0%-61.7%) below the INR range. Neither the univariate nor the multivariate analysis identified genetic or nongenetic factors associated with the time spent within, above, or below the INR range.
PMC3384479.md:A limitation of our study is that most of the patients were whites and therefore our results may not apply to non-white children, because the allele frequencies of SNPs associated with warfarin dose requirements vary across ethnic groups. Another limitation is the small number of fluindione-treated patients, which did not allow us to build a dose prediction model for fluindione. Finally, the patients spent more than 80% of the time within the INR range. This may have impaired our ability to identify factors that significantly influenced the times spent within, above, or below the INR range.
PMC4209173.md:Warfarin, a synthetic coumarin used for anticoagulation, has a narrow therapeutic index and exhibits wide inter-individual variation in the maintenance dose required (0.5 mg/day to 20 mg/day). Proportionally, far fewer children are treated with warfarin than adults. Indications for paediatric use include treatment of thromboembolic disorders, prophylaxis for heart valve replacement, following cavopulmonary shunts and completion of the Fontan circulation in patients with complex congenital heart disease ^1^. Warfarin requires regular therapeutic monitoring through the use of the International Normalised Ratio (INR). The INR required for successful anticoagulation varies by indication for anticoagulation ([Supplementary Data Table 1](#SD1)), following national recommendations ^2^.
PMC5859345.md:Keywords: : allelic frequency, CYP2C9, Gaza strip, IWPC algorithm, pharmacogenetics, vitamin K antagonist, VKORC1, warfarin
PMC6714673.md:The goal of pharmacogenetics is to predict patient’s response to a specific drug, based on the genetic variations, in order to deliver the best possible medical treatment. Prediction of the drug response of an individual can increase the success of therapies and reduce the incidence of adverse side effects. Till date, numerous single-nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) are identified in genes coding for liver enzymes responsible for drug deposition and pharmacokinetics. Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations in which knowledge of pharmacogenomics can deliver safe personalized dosing.
PMC6714673.md:Many studies have reported that *VKORC1* and *CYP2C9* polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of warfarin dose requirement, but the present study shows that integrating patient’s diet and age can marginally improve the power of dose prediction. It is reported that a vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist) and may need high warfarin maintenance dose,^23,24,25^ whereas Ford et al in a review of literature concluded that vitamin K supplementation may reduce variation in drug response caused by dietary intake.^26^ Few other studies have suggested that patients on warfarin need to maintain consistent dietary vitamin K intake to achieve more stable anticoagulation response.^23,27^ In the present study, nonvegetarian patients were found to require higher warfarin dose than the vegetarian patients.

---

Here are the complete results for all four queries:

---

## RESULT 1: Papers NOT containing rs9923231 or VKORC1

The following 10 papers (out of 41 total) contain neither "rs9923231" nor "VKORC1":

```
/app/papers/PMC2014334.md
/app/papers/PMC2745926.md

/app/papers/PMC6385304.md
```

---

## RESULT 2: Titles and Abstracts of the 10 Non-rs9923231 Papers

### PMC2014334
**Title:** Frequency of cytochrome P450 CYP2C9 variants in a Turkish population and functional relevance for phenytoin
**Authors:** Aynacioglu et al., British Journal of Clinical Pharmacology, 1999
**Abstract:** Studies CYP2C9*2 and *3 allele frequencies in 499 Turkish subjects and correlates genotype with phenytoin pharmacokinetics. No mention of VKORC1 or rs9923231. The anticoagulant mention is only incidental (EDTA blood collection tubes). The only warfarin mention is in references about CYP2C9's role in S-warfarin metabolism. The paper's focus is entirely on phenytoin as a CYP2C9 probe drug.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2745926
**Title:** Nitric oxide synthase (NOS3) variants and disease-free survival among treated and untreated breast cancer patients in a SWOG Clinical Trial (S8897)
**Authors:** Choi et al., Clinical Cancer Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines relationships between disease-free survival and variants in NOS3, NQO1, NQO2 and CBR3 in breast cancer patients enrolled in a Southwest Oncology Group clinical trial. Finds a significant interaction between NOS3 Glu298Asp and -786 polymorphisms and adjuvant chemotherapy outcomes. Entirely focused on breast cancer and oxidative stress genes.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2976715
**Title:** Role of Organic Cation Transporter 3 (SLC22A3) and Its Missense Variants in the Pharmacologic Action of Metformin
**Authors:** Chen et al., Pharmacogenetics and Genomics, 2010
**Abstract:** Determines that OCT3 plays a role in metformin's pharmacologic action (AMPK activation) in skeletal muscle. Identifies and functionally characterizes 6 novel missense variants of OCT3. Topic is metformin and organic cation transport, with no connection to warfarin or anticoagulation.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC2993460
**Title:** Ecto-5'-Nucleotidase and Thiopurine Cellular Circulation: Association with Cytotoxicity
**Authors:** Li et al., Drug Metabolism and Disposition, 2010
**Abstract:** Studies the thiopurine pathway (6-MP and 6-TG) in 194 lymphoblastoid cell lines. Identifies a "thiopurine cellular circulation" involving NT5E, ABCC4, and nucleoside transporters. Entirely focused on leukemia chemotherapy pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3505921
**Title:** Influence of Methylenetetrahydrofolate Reductase C677T, A1298C, and G80A Polymorphisms on the Survival of Pediatric Patients with Acute Lymphoblastic Leukemia
**Authors:** Deus et al., Leukemia Research and Treatment, 2012
**Abstract:** Analyzes MTHFR and RFC1 polymorphisms and overall survival in 126 pediatric ALL patients treated with methotrexate. The C677T TT genotype shows better survival (~98%) while 677CC shows lower survival. Entirely in the ALL/methotrexate/folate metabolism space.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3691683
**Title:** Initial Evidence of an Association between OPRM1 and Adolescent Alcohol Misuse
**Authors:** Miranda et al., Alcoholism: Clinical and Experimental Research, 2009
**Abstract:** Examines the OPRM1 A118G SNP (rs1799971) and alcohol use disorder in 187 adolescents. Finds that 51.9% of youth with AUD carry at least one G allele vs. 16.3% of controls. Topic is opioid receptor genetics and adolescent alcohol misuse.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC3778124
**Title:** Variation in the Alpha 5 Nicotinic Acetylcholine Receptor Subunit Gene Predicts Cigarette Smoking Intensity as a Function of Nicotine Content
**Authors:** MacQueen et al., The Pharmacogenomics Journal, 2013
**Abstract:** Investigates CHRNA5 polymorphisms and smoking topography in 66 smokers. Finds that genotype at rs16969968 predicts nicotine titration (puff volume). Topic is nicotine addiction and CHRNA5 genetics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC4567270
**Title:** Cholelithiasis and Nephrolithiasis in HIV-Positive Patients in the Era of Combination Antiretroviral Therapy
**Authors:** Lin et al., PLoS ONE, 2015
**Abstract:** Retrospective review of 910 HIV-positive patients examining prevalence and risk factors for gallstones and kidney stones. Ritonavir-boosted atazanavir for >2 years is associated with cholelithiasis. Examines UGT1A1*28 and MDR1 G2677T/A polymorphisms (not VKORC1). Topic is HIV/antiretroviral pharmacogenomics.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC5464525
**Title:** Genetic variants in ALDH1B1 and alcohol dependence risk in a British and Irish population
**Authors:** Way et al., PLoS ONE, 2017
**Abstract:** Uses bioinformatic techniques to characterize ALDH1B1 missense variants (rs2073478, rs2228093, rs4878199, rs142427338) and genotypes 1478 alcohol-dependent cases vs. 1254 controls. No significant allelic associations found. Topic is alcohol metabolism genetics (ALDH/ADH) and alcohol dependence risk.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

### PMC6385304
**Title:** Polymorphism A118G of opioid receptor mu 1 (OPRM1) is associated with emergence of suicidal ideation at antidepressant onset
**Authors:** Nobile et al., Scientific Reports, 2019
**Abstract:** Studies rs1799971 (OPRM1) and rs105660 (OPRK1) in 3566 depressed outpatients receiving tianeptine. Finds AA genotype of rs1799971 associated with treatment-emergent suicidal ideation (OR=1.93). Topic is opioidergic system, depression, and suicidality.
**Conclusion: NOT relevant to rs9923231.**

---

## RESULT 3: Drug Names Beyond Warfarin in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

There are 31 papers mentioning VKORC1/rs9923231. Here are the substantive (non-reference) matches for non-warfarin VKA drugs and coumarin-class terms:

### Papers with substantive body-text mentions:

**PMC3384479** — "Vitamin K antagonists in children with heart disease..." — THE richest source:
- **Fluindione** mentioned extensively as the actual drug used in 35 of 118 children studied
  - "warfarin (n = 83) or fluindione (n = 35) maintenance dose"
  - Mean weekly fluindione dose: 123.1 ± 52.2 mg (range 45–250 mg); 4.07 ± 2.68 mg/kg
  - "The VKORC1 genotype was the only variable significantly associated with the weekly fluindione maintenance dose (P = .0363)"
  - "The effect of CYP2C9 on non-coumarin VKAs such as fluindione is unclear"
  - "Two patients receiving acenocoumarol were not analyzed" (acenocoumarol excluded)
  - Section heading: "Influence of genetic and nongenetic variables on maintenance dose in fluindione-treated patients"
  - VKORC1 rs9923231 directly associated with fluindione dose

**PMC3175513:**
- "The coumarins are the most used class of oral anticoagulants, to which warfarin belongs"
- "SNPs...affect the pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics of coumarins, respectively"

**PMC2911553:**
- "Warfarin, a coumarin derivative, exerts its anticoagulant effect by inhibiting vitamin K epoxide reductase (VKORC1)"

**PMC3125052:**
- "Warfarin/Acitrom and related coumarins are widely used in thromboembolic prophylaxis"
- Acitrom (acenocoumarol equivalent used in India) is the second drug studied alongside warfarin in this paper

**PMC4209173:**
- "Warfarin, a synthetic coumarin used for anticoagulation"

**PMC5859345:**
- Keywords include: "vitamin K antagonist, VKORC1, warfarin"

**PMC6714673:**
- "Warfarin (coumarin) therapy is one of the many clinical situations..."
- "prevent the action of warfarin (vitamin K antagonist)"

### References-only mentions (cited drugs, not studied in the paper itself):
- **Acenocoumarol**: cited in PMC2515139, PMC2586955, PMC3038469, PMC4296935, PMC5006145 (all reference citations, e.g., Schalekamp et al. on VKORC1/CYP2C9 and acenocoumarol; Bodin et al. on acenocoumarol sensitivity)
- **Phenprocoumon**: cited in PMC2586955 (Schalekamp et al. on VKORC1/CYP2C9 and phenprocoumon), PMC4296935 (Luxembourg et al. on phenprocoumon therapy)
- **Coumarin (general)**: referenced in PMC3718044, PMC3753327, PMC4702374, PMC4706412, PMC5508045, PMC6489578

**Summary of drugs found:**
| Drug | Status in papers |
|---|---|
| Warfarin | Primary drug studied across most papers |
| Fluindione | Actually studied in PMC3384479 (35 patients); VKORC1 significantly associated with dose |
| Acenocoumarol | Studied in PMC3125052 (as "Acitrom"); cited in references of 5+ papers |
| Phenprocoumon | Referenced only (cited papers) in PMC2586955, PMC4296935 |
| Coumarin (class) | Referenced descriptively in multiple papers |

---

## RESULT 4: Phenotype/Dosage-Related Terms in VKORC1/rs9923231-Positive Papers

Key substantive matches across papers (filtered for body text, not reference URLs):

**PMC2515139:**
- "Both VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms independently correlate with warfarin dose and other clinical outcomes such as **time to stabilized dose, bleeding events, and time within the target therapeutic range**"
- "Combined polymorphisms...explain approximately 30% of the variance in the stabilized warfarin dose distribution"

**PMC2911553:**
- "the most commonly used oral anticoagulant for patients with **mechanical heart valve replacement** (MHVR) to prevent **thromboembolism**"
- "the narrow therapeutic index and the wide inter-individual variation...to minimize the adverse events and to shorten the time to reach the target INR"

**PMC3125052:**
- "The subtherapeutic and supratherapeutic use of these drugs is associated with an increased risk of clot formation and **bleeding**"
- "the dose requirements of these anticoagulants vary between individuals"
- "interindividual variations in the VKORC1-1639 G>A gene polymorphism has been linked to differences in the **dose requirements** for oral anticoagulants"
- "patients taking Acitrom after **valve replacement surgery**"

**PMC3175513:**
- "SNPs in CYP2C9 and VKORC1 genes...are strongly associated with warfarin dose"
- "rs9923231...associated with a reduction of approximately 30% in warfarin dose"
- "The dose to achieve the target INR ranges from an average of 4 mg to 80 mg weekly"

**PMC3384479:**
- "VKAs have a narrow **therapeutic range** and considerable inter- and intra-individual **dose-response variability**"
- "height, target INR and VKORC1 and CYP2C9 genotypes were the main determinants of **warfarin dose requirement**, accounting for 48.1%, 4.4%, 18.2%, and 2.0% of variability"
- "prevent **thromboembolism** after **cardiac valve replacement**, total cavopulmonary connection, dilated cardiomyopathy, coronary aneurysms after Kawasaki disease"
- "patients had **mitral valve replacement** with a target INR of 3.3"
- "rs9923231 located in the functional promoter of VKORC1 require substantially lower doses"

**PMC4209173:**
- "Indications for paediatric use include treatment of **thromboembolic disorders**, prophylaxis for **heart valve replacement**"
- "**therapeutic monitoring** through the use of the International Normalised Ratio"
- "wide inter-individual variation in the maintenance dose required (0.5 mg/day to 20 mg/day)"

**PMC6714673:**
- "VKORC1 and CYP2C9 polymorphisms are the major predicting factors of **warfarin dose requirement**"
- "vitamin K–rich diet can prevent the action of warfarin...and may need high warfarin maintenance dose"

**Key phenotype categories found across all 31 VKORC1-positive papers:**
| Phenotype | Evidence |
|---|---|
| Warfarin/VKA dose requirement | Core phenotype in virtually all papers; quantitative dose (mg/week) |
| Dose variability | Consistently described; VKORC1+CYP2C9 explain 30-55% of variance |
| Therapeutic range / INR control | Time in therapeutic range (TTR) mentioned; INR targets specified |
| Bleeding risk | Referenced as key adverse outcome of over-anticoagulation |
| Thromboembolism | Referenced as indication for therapy and outcome of under-anticoagulation |
| Heart valve replacement | Frequent indication (mechanical valve, mitral/aortic valve) |
| Atrial fibrillation | Appears as an indication in several papers |
| Time to stable dose | Mentioned as clinical outcome in PMC2515139 |
| Over-anticoagulation | Referenced in citations (Schalekamp acenocoumarol paper title) |

---

**Summary:**

1. All 10 papers listed in Result 1 are confirmed NOT relevant to rs9923231 — they cover CYP2C9/phenytoin pharmacogenetics (PMC2014334), breast cancer survival (PMC2745926), metformin/OCT3 transport (PMC2976715), thiopurine/leukemia (PMC2993460), MTHFR/ALL survival (PMC3505921), OPRM1/alcohol in adolescents (PMC3691683), CHRNA5/nicotine (PMC3778124), HIV/atazanavir stones (PMC4567270), ALDH1B1/alcohol dependence (PMC5464525), and OPRM1/suicidal ideation (PMC6385304).

2. The 31 VKORC1-positive papers cover warfarin and VKA dosing pharmacogenetics. Beyond warfarin, the key non-warfarin drugs mentioned in substantive body text are: **fluindione** (actually studied in PMC3384479, with VKORC1 directly linked to its dose), **acenocoumarol/Acitrom** (studied in PMC3125052; cited in references elsewhere), and **phenprocoumon** (referenced in citations only). The primary phenotypes documented are warfarin/VKA dose requirement, INR control/therapeutic range, bleeding, thromboembolism, and cardiac indications (heart valve replacement, atrial fibrillation).
